Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty intense but not always consistent. I'd dedicate around 22 hours a week, mostly in longer blocks when I could really focus. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time creating diagrams, color-coded notes, and mind maps. I tried to spread things out, but sometimes life got in the way and I'd have to cram everything into a few days. The seven online courses I was taking made it challenging to keep everything organized, but I developed a system using different colored folders for each subject.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to have some regularity, like studying in the afternoons when my mind felt sharpest, but deadlines would completely throw that off. When multiple assignments were due around the same time, I'd shift into survival mode and study whenever I could. Some weeks I was super disciplined, other weeks it was chaos. Being 18 and still figuring out university life meant I was constantly adjusting my approach.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or materials posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to see discussion posts from classmates or to check my grades. Deadlines were a big motivator too – if something was due soon, I'd be on there making sure I hadn't missed any instructions. Honestly, sometimes I logged in out of anxiety, just to make sure I wasn't falling behind on something important.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. With my attendance being what it was, the LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially during busy assessment periods. But it never felt natural or automatic – it always required a conscious decision to log in. I think if the interface was more engaging visually, I might have used it more consistently since I learn better that way.
Q5: The video content was definitely the most useful for me – I watched over 169 minutes worth because I really absorb information better when I can see it explained visually. Discussion forums were also helpful because I could see how other students understood concepts, which gave me different perspectives. Any resources with diagrams, charts, or visual representations clicked with me immediately. I also appreciated when instructors posted annotated slides because those helped me understand the key points without having to read dense text.
Q6: I often skipped the lengthy reading materials, especially PDFs that were just walls of text. My visual learning style made those really tedious to get through. I also didn't engage much with some of the supplementary resources that weren't directly tied to assessments – time was limited and I had to prioritize. Some of the older forum threads I'd just skim past because they felt irrelevant by the time I saw them.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to my grades or to understanding something I was struggling with. Visual elements made a huge difference – if something had good diagrams or videos, I was all in. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because those 48 peer interactions I had really helped solidify my understanding. If something felt like busywork with no clear purpose, I'd deprioritize it quickly.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. With seven courses and medium stress levels, I had to be strategic about where I put my energy. Required activities got done no matter what, but optional ones only happened if I had extra time or was genuinely curious. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional stuff, but I had to be realistic about my capacity. The optional activities that were visual or interactive were more tempting though.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them on my tablet during commutes or when my internet was acting up. I created tons of handwritten notes with diagrams and flowcharts that I'd review constantly. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My desk was covered in printed mind maps and color-coded summaries that represented hours of work the LMS never saw.
Q10: There were many times I'd be deep in study mode with my physical notes spread out everywhere, making connections between concepts, and the LMS would show nothing. I'd spend hours redrawing diagrams from memory to test myself, or I'd be explaining concepts to my roommate to reinforce my learning. Group study sessions at the library were really productive but completely invisible online. Sometimes my best learning happened while just thinking through problems during a walk.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, then download it and rewatch it multiple times offline while taking notes. Same with lecture slides – I'd access them once to download, then print them out and annotate them by hand for weeks. The LMS would show one access, but that material might have been central to hours of my studying. It was more efficient for me to work with physical copies I could highlight and draw on.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. My stress levels would spike and I'd feel this moderate anxiety creeping in about falling behind. Sometimes I'd feel overwhelmed by having seven courses demanding attention simultaneously. The CBT sessions I had helped me work through some of those moments, but there were times when I just felt mentally exhausted and couldn't engage properly with anything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities – like family stuff or just basic life maintenance – would eat into my study time. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily because facing them felt overwhelming. My task completion rate suffered because sometimes I just couldn't push through the mental fog.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other assignments or just zoning out from exhaustion. Sometimes I'd scroll through materials without actually processing the information. There were sessions where I'd spend an hour on the LMS but couldn't tell you what I actually learned. It's frustrating because the time was spent, but the engagement wasn't really there.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments that felt too challenging or that I didn't understand well enough to start. The anxiety about doing them poorly made me avoid them, which ironically made the anxiety worse. Sometimes I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure to justify procrastination. Looking back, my 5.2% assignment completion rate shows how much I struggled with this. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it was still hard.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd be relatively quiet on the platform, then suddenly very active as due dates approached. My login frequency would spike around assessment periods. The pressure of deadlines was both motivating and stressful – it got me working, but not always in the most effective way. I wish I'd been better at spreading out the work, but the urgency of deadlines was what really got me moving.
Q17: Feedback was huge for my motivation. When I got positive feedback, I'd feel energized and engage more with similar activities. Constructive criticism helped me understand where to focus my efforts. But waiting for feedback was anxiety-inducing, and sometimes the delay made me disengage temporarily. My exam score of 79 and assignment scores around 82 showed me I was capable, which kept me going even when things felt hard.
Q18: The course structure definitely influenced how I participated. When modules were clearly organized with visual elements, I engaged more readily. Courses that frontloaded all the reading without visual breaks were harder for me to stay connected with. The flexibility of online courses was good for my schedule, but sometimes that same flexibility made it easy to fall behind. Having seven courses meant I gravitated toward the ones with structures that matched my learning style.
Q19: Visual elements made engagement so much easier – courses with good video content, infographics, and clear visual organization kept me coming back. What made it harder was dense text-heavy content without visual aids, or confusing navigation that made finding materials frustrating. Discussion forums were engaging when they were active, but felt pointless when participation was low. Clear connections between activities and assessments helped me prioritize effectively.
Q20: I think that happens when students are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually complete the work. Maybe they're confused about requirements, or they're anxious about submitting imperfect work. It could also be that they're using the LMS to study but doing assignments elsewhere or procrastinating on the final submission step. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of struggle – clicking around trying to understand what's expected.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience pretty well. My attendance and LMS engagement numbers look low, but I still earned a Merit grade with decent exam and assignment scores. I did a lot of learning offline with my visual notes and diagrams. I was efficient when I did engage – I knew what I needed and got it done. Sometimes less visible activity means more focused, intentional studying rather than aimless browsing.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when students are active, what resources they access, and basic completion rates. But they miss the quality of engagement – whether someone is actually learning or just going through the motions. They can't capture offline studying, mental effort, or the emotional experience of learning. My 169 minutes of video watching shows quantity, but not that I was actively pausing, rewinding, and taking detailed visual notes.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks for grades. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts, and feeling motivated to learn more. True engagement for me involves creating those visual representations that help concepts stick. It's also about feeling connected to the course community through discussions and peer interactions. Engagement is mental presence, not just physical or digital presence.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience – how I pushed through stress and anxiety to still perform well. It can't capture the hours I spent creating hand-drawn diagrams and mind maps that were central to my learning. My peer study sessions, the concepts I explained to friends, the moments of genuine understanding – none of that shows up. It also misses my emotional journey, including the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety and keep going when things got tough.
